California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
April r-2, 1990
vincent F. Biondo, Jr,
city Attorney
12o0 carlsbad Village Drive
carLsbad, cA 9 2 008-1989
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-071
Dear Ur. Biondo:
This is in response to your request for clarification of the
inforrnal assj,stance provided to you in our Advice Letter No. I-89-
646 (copy enclosed) regarding your responsibi lities under the
Political Reforn Act (the trActx).r Since your request does not
involve a specific pending decision, we are continuing to treat
your request as one for informal assistance pursuant to Requlation
18329(c) (copy enclosed) .2 By way of background the facts in I-
a9-646 were as f oIlor./s:
The San Diego cas and Electric company
(r'sDG&Ert) owns and operates a major electrical
generating facility, 
the Encina Power Plant,
located in the city of Carlsbad (the "city"). 
The
city has received formal notice that SDG&E has
asked the state Energy Cornrnission for approval to
construct a new plant at one of five locations, one
of which is at Encina. It is your understanding
that SDG&E is required to propose five possible
sites for the proposed plant in its application to
the Energy Commission. The city has land use
authority over the site of the proposed p1ant. You
expect that at some point SDG&E nust seek a land
use permit fron the city if the decision is made to
1 Go.,".rrr.nt Code Secti,ons SLOOO-91-015. AIl statutory references
are to the Goverhrnent Code unless otherwise indicated. Comrnission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18OO0, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 5 of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83L14,. 2 CaI. Code of Regs.
Section r.8329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8oo . P.o. Box 8o7 o sacramento cA 95go4-ogoz . (916) 3 22-5660


Our File No. 
エー90-07■
Page 2
construct the plant at Encina. You expect that the
city council will take a position regarding the
proposed plant and direct you to present that
position during the hearings before the Energy
Corornission on sDG & E's proposed plant. 
you may
also participate in advising Danagement on the
options they rrill present to the city council to
aid it in formulating its position rrith respect to
the proposed plant.
You have no financial interest in SDG&E.
However, you have an investDent in Southern
CaLifornia Edison colpany ('rEdisonrr). Edison is
traded on the New York stock Exchange. SDG&E and
Edison have entered into an agreenent for a rnerger
subject to certain reg'ulatory approvals. An SDG&E
spokesman has said there is no connection between
the nerger and the proposed plant, but that
approval of the rnerger rrould delay the need for the
power plant by approxinately three years.
That letter concluded that you may participate in decisions
regarding the proposed p1ant, and present the city council's
position before the Energy Commission unless the decisions are
1ikeIy to have a reasonably foreseeable and naterial financial
effect on Edison.
In the second paragraph of your letter seeking clarification,
you questioned how your efforts to stop the construction of the
i:-..,€ "an have rra rniterial financial effect on SDG&E. "3 As stated
3 Yo, have stated that it is your understanding that if the
Energy Cornrnission, the Public Utilities 
Commission, and the
coastal Commission approve the Plant, it will be constructed
notr.rithstanding carlsbad's opposition. The Act does not focus
only on the final decision. Section 87100 prohibits a public
official fron attempting to influence the decision. Regulation
18700.1 (copy enclosed) states in relevant part:
with regard to a governnental decision which
is within or before an agency not covered by
subsection (a), the official 
is attempting to use
his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decj.sion, the official acts or purports to act on
behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her
agency to any member, officer, employee or
consultant of an agency. Such actions include, but
are not linited to the use of official stationery.
Regulation 18700. 1(c) .
Since you nay be representing the city council,s position


Our File No. I-9 0-071
Page 3
above, our letter concluded that you Day participate unless the
decision tras a material financial effect on Edison; our conclusion
did not focus on the rnaterial financial effect on SDG&E.
You state the guestion in the second paragraph of your letter
seeking clarification as follot s:
will Dy activities in carlsbad, as the city
pursues its efforts to stop constructj.on of the
plant, have Ia reasonably foreseeable rnaterial
financiall effect and how am I supposed to
deternine it?
section 8?100 prohibits any public official 
from rnaking,
participating in, or using his official position to influence a
governnental deci,sion in which he knows or has reason to know he
has a financial interest. 
An official 
has a financial interest in
a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable frorn its effect
on the public aenerally, on the official 
or a member of his
imrnediate family, { or on:
Any business entity in which the public
offici,al has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,0o0) or more.
Section I7L03 (a) .
You are a public official. 
(Section 82048.) You have an
investment interest in Edison presumably worth more than $1,000.
(section a2o34. ) Accordingly, you may not participate in, or
attempt to influence any decision which wiII have a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect, distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally, on Edison.
The focus of the conf lict-of-interest 
provisions of the IaLr
is to ensure that decisions nade by public officials are not
influenced by their financial interests. 
For example, if
decisions regarding the plant proposed by SDG&E could have a
beneficial or negative effect on Edison, then the decisions of a
public official with a financial interest in Edison may be colored
by that interest. 
Whereas it is true that rnost officials 
would
not permit their financial interests to influence their decisions,
the Act seeks to prevent even the appearance of a conflict of
before these different agencies, you are attempting to use
position to influence a governmental decision. You may do
unless the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and
rnaterial financial- effect, distinguishable from the effect
public aenerally, on Edison.
4 An official,s 
ttinmediate familyt' includes his spouse and
dependent children. 
(Section 82029 . )
your
SO,
on the


Our File No. I-9 0-071
Page 4
interest. 
It is towaids this end that the Act, in section 87103,
sets forth the threshold level of financial interests which
reguire further inquiry, to deterruine lrhether certain decisions
may have a reasonabLy foreseeable and rnaterial financial effect,
distinguishable froD the effect on the public Aenerally, on the
official's 
financial interest. 
The commission has interpreted the
terns IgesenaLly f oreseeable,@, 
and
effect on the public qenerally.
In your letter you stated that approval of the merger of
Edison and SDG&E rrould delay the need for the porirer plant by
approxirnately three years. This lrouId suggest that subsequent to
the merger, Edison would be able to provide the energTy Lrhich the
proposed plant is intended to provide, This in turn would suggest
that Edison has excess generating capacity at the present time. A
decision which would result in the utilization of this excess
generating capacity rrroutd have sorne financial effect on Edison. A
decision denying approval of the proposed plant r.rould appear to
enhance the probabitity of utilization of that excess capacity.
Accordingly, an official 
who has a financial interest in Edison
should not participate in rnaking, or attenpt to influence' any
decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable and material
financial effect on Edison which is distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally.
whether the decision rrtill have a reasonably foreseeable
econornic effect on Edison is determined by the circurnstances of
the case. Had there been no merger agreement pending between
SDG&E and Edison, Edison's position would be sirnilar to that of
any other utility 
in the region with sorne excess generating
capacity. However, because of the pending merger aqreement,
Edison is in a unique position to benefit from a decision which
would enable it utilize any excess capacity. ?he guestion of
foreseeability hinges on this pending merger agreement.
In In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops. L98 (copy enclosed), the
commission provided some guidelines to deterrnine whether the
effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable. A director of a
water district 
rras the executive vice-president and a minority
shareholder of a business entity r.rhich did over half of its
business in the r.rater district. 
The business entity supplied
ready rnix concrete, building materials, and rnajor appliances in
that area. The issue focused on the possibility of a conflict of
interest involving that director of the water dj,strict if an
applicant for a project, requesting a variance fron a moratorium
on new water connections, came before the Board of Directors of
the water district.
The Commission considered various scenarios involving the
director's business entity and the proposed project. 
one of the
situations the Conmission analyzed was that the business entity
vas preparing, or had made a bid, to supply one or Dore of the
products to the proposed project, but no award had been rnade. The


our Pile No. I-9 0-071
Page 5
Comnission concluded that a financial effect was reasonably
foreseeable. The Conmission stated:
As a general rule .., uhen the bid is urade
with a serious hope that the contract wiII be
awarded to lthe business entity], we thi-nk a
financial effect on lthe business entity] is
reasonably foreseeable even if there is substantial
conpetition. The statute requires foreseeability,
not certainty. 
r'urthermore, the fact that a
seriously competitive bid on the project is being
prepared or has been nade is likely to focus the
attention of the Director on the fact that he may
benefit if a variance is granted. The ultinate
test is whether the elernent of foreseeability,
together with the other elements discussed earlier,
is present to the point that the official's
ttunquatj,f ied devotion to his public dutyt' night be
inpaired. People v. Darbv, 114 cal. App.2d 412,
433 (1952) . Under the circumstances described ...,
we conclude that the financial effect on [the
business entityl, is reasonabl-y foreseeable and
that [the directorl , therefore, must not vote or
participate in the variance decisj,on.
In re Thornerr EIJEEB, 1 FPPC
ops. at 206.
we believe the situation regarding Edison's merger with SDG&E
is. sirnilar. 
Although, as you have described, there is substantial
opposition to the merger, it is also true that the tlro conpanies,
Edison and SDG&E, have entered into an agreenent for a nerger
subject to certain regulatory approvals. ft appears unlikely that
Edison and SDG&E vould have committed the necessary resources,
with the consequent expenditure of time and money, if they did not
believe they would be successful in accornplishing the merger. As
stated in my original letter to you:
SDG&E and Edison have entered into an
agreement for a merger subject to certain
regrlrlatory approvals. The threshold question is
r.rhether there is a substantial likelihood that the
decision regarding approval of the plant rrill have
an economic effect on Edison. If the merger
process is very preliminary and if there is very
Iittle 
certainty that the two companies will merge,
it would be difficult 
to clairn that there is such a
substantial likelihood of an economic effect on
Edison. On the other hand, if the merger process
is further alonq and there is a greater degree of
certainty that the two companies witl indeed merge,
then there would be a substantial likelihood, that
decisions regarding the plant vould affect Edison.
In this case the Board of Directors and the


、     Our File No. 
■-90-07■
・ ・    Page 6
KED 3 JSA: aa
Enc losures
shareholders of Edison and SDG&E have already
approved the merger. There is no inforrnation that
suggests that the nerger process has hit a
regulatory roadblock. Under the cj,rcurostances it
appears that there is a substantial Likelihood that
the Derger will occur, and, therefore, that the
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
econonic effect on Edison.
Therefore, we believe it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision regarding approval of the proposed plant lrill 
have a
financial effect on Edison.
Finally, you have also requested additiona). guidance on
determining materiality. 
You state that utitity 
rate making is an
extremely complex matter, and that you are at a loss to understand
how you can possibly determine whether the effect of the decision
is naterial. 
We synpathize with your dilemrna, but unfortunately,
lre are unable to provide you with any specific guidance in
determininqr whether the effect of the decision on Edison is
material, we generally advice public officials 
who are faced with
such decisions that they nake a good faith effort to ascertain the
magnitude of the effect that nay result fron a specific decisj.on.
This night include contacting an expert in that field to determine
lrhether the effect vould exceed the monetary standards outLined in
Regulation L8702.2 (copy enclosed).
Please recognize that our conclusion is based on the facts
that you have provided. The discussion is designed to assist you
in deterrnining whether or not you have a conflict of interest.
There rnay be other facts available to you which, when considered
with the above, may lead you to a contrary conclusion, Since any
conclusion is a product of the underlying facts, it is not
uncomnon for a conclusion to change when additional facts,
prevj,ously not obvious, reveal themselves and, therefore, affect
the entire analysis.
I trust this letter has provided you with the clarification
you requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at (915) 322-5901.
Sincerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
ceneral counsel
